skill.name: azure-data-tables-java
skill.description: Build table storage applications with Azure Tables SDK for Java. Supports Azure Table Storage and Cosmos DB Table API for NoSQL key-value data.
skill.summary: Azure Tables SDK for Java skill enabling table storage operations including CRUD, queries, and batch transactions.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is safe for publication. All static analysis findings are false positives. The external_commands detections are Java code examples in markdown format, not shell execution. URL references are environment variable documentation examples, not hardcoded endpoints. The cryptographic and system reconnaissance flags are incorrect pattern matches on documentation text and getter method names.

content.user_title: Build Azure Table Storage Apps with Java
content.value_statement: Need to store structured NoSQL data at scale? This skill helps you implement table storage solutions using Azure Tables SDK for Java with proper authentication and efficient query patterns.
actual_capabilities: Create and manage Azure Table Storage clients with connection strings, shared keys, SAS tokens, or DefaultAzureCredential
Perform CRUD operations on table entities including create, read, update, upsert, and delete
Execute filtered queries using OData filter expressions with partition key optimization
Submit batch transactions for atomic multi-entity operations within the same partition
Implement typed entity classes that implement the TableEntity interface
Handle TableServiceException errors with proper status code interpretation
limitations: Requires Azure Table Storage or Cosmos DB Table API account with valid credentials
Entities limited to 1MB for Storage accounts or 2MB for Cosmos DB
Batch operations require all entities to share the same partition key
Does not support Cosmos DB Table API with DefaultAzureCredential (Storage only)

use_cases.title: Application State Storage
use_cases.description: Store user sessions, application settings, or feature flags with fast partition-based lookups
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers building scalable web applications

use_cases.title: IoT Telemetry Data
use_cases.description: Ingest and query sensor data using device ID as partition key with timestamp row keys

use_cases.title: Product Catalog Management
use_cases.description: Maintain product inventory with category-based partitions and efficient price/availability queries

prompt_templates.title: Initialize Table Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TableServiceClient using connection string authentication, then create a table called 'inventory' if it does not exist.

prompt_templates.title: CRUD Entity Operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create a new entity with partition key 'products', row key 'laptop-001', and properties Name, Price, and Quantity. Then retrieve and update the price.

prompt_templates.title: Filtered Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Query all entities where PartitionKey equals 'electronics' and Price is greater than 100, returning only Name and Price properties with a limit of 10 results.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Transaction
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a batch transaction that inserts three entities with the same partition key 'batch' and row keys 'row1', 'row2', and 'row3' atomically.

output_examples.input: Create an entity and handle conflict errors

best_practices: Design partition keys to distribute queries evenly and avoid hotspots
Always filter by PartitionKey in queries for optimal performance
Use batch transactions for atomic updates to entities sharing the same partition key
anti_patterns: Using timestamps or sequential values as partition keys causes uneven load distribution
Querying without PartitionKey filter results in full table scans and poor performance
Storing entities larger than size limits causes failures - keep under 1MB for Storage or 2MB for Cosmos

faq.question: What is the difference between Azure Table Storage and Cosmos DB Table API?
faq.answer: Azure Table Storage offers cost-effective key-value storage with 1MB entity limit. Cosmos DB Table API provides global distribution, lower latency, and 2MB entity limit with higher cost.

faq.question: How do I choose a good partition key?
faq.answer: Select a property with high cardinality that distributes queries evenly. Common patterns include user IDs, device IDs, or category codes based on your access patterns.

faq.question: Can I update just one property of an entity?
faq.answer: Yes, use updateEntity with TableEntityUpdateMode.MERGE to update only specified properties, or REPLACE to overwrite the entire entity.

faq.question: What authentication methods are supported?
faq.answer: Connection strings, shared keys, SAS tokens, and DefaultAzureCredential (for Table Storage only, not Cosmos DB Table API).

faq.question: How many entities can I include in a batch transaction?
faq.answer: Batch operations support up to 100 entities per transaction, all sharing the same partition key and within 4MB total payload size.

faq.question: What does a 409 conflict error mean?
faq.answer: A 409 status code indicates the entity already exists. Use upsertEntity instead of createEntity, or handle the exception and retry with updateEntity.
